company_scope_thresholds.description: Which companies fall within the directive's scope based on employee count and turnover thresholds.
company_scope_thresholds.salience: Industry lobbies for higher thresholds to exclude more firms; civil society and trade unions push for lower thresholds for maximum coverage. SME associations fear indirect burden even when formally excluded.

value_chain_depth.description: How far along the upstream and downstream value chain companies must conduct due diligence.
value_chain_depth.salience: Large multinationals resist full-chain liability due to complexity and cost; NGOs and unions demand full upstream coverage to capture abuses at raw material extraction level.

civil_liability.description: Whether and under what conditions companies face civil liability for adverse impacts in their value chains.
civil_liability.salience: Business groups seek to limit or eliminate private enforcement; human rights NGOs and plaintiff lawyers see civil liability as the directive's core enforcement lever. A major fault line between industry and civil society.

director_duty_of_care.description: Requirements placed on company directors and boards to integrate sustainability into governance and remuneration.
director_duty_of_care.salience: Corporate governance lobby opposes fiduciary duty extensions; institutional investors split between those favouring ESG integration and those fearing litigation exposure. Member states differ on company law traditions.

climate_transition_plans.description: Whether companies must adopt and implement Paris-aligned climate transition plans as part of due diligence.
climate_transition_plans.salience: Energy-intensive industries and fossil fuel companies resist binding transition plan obligations; climate NGOs demand science-based targets with enforcement. Commission's climate ambition directly at stake.

high_risk_sector_differentiation.description: Whether specific high-risk sectors such as textiles, agriculture, and minerals face stricter or earlier-entry due diligence obligations.
high_risk_sector_differentiation.salience: Sector-specific rules create competitive inequality; textile and agri-food industries lobby against stricter obligations while NGOs push for sector differentiation to target worst abusers.

third_country_company_coverage.description: Conditions under which non-EU companies generating revenue in the EU are subject to equivalent obligations.
third_country_company_coverage.salience: EU industry demands a level playing field requiring non-EU rivals to comply; third countries and global trade bodies warn of protectionism and WTO incompatibility.

stakeholder_engagement_requirements.description: Obligations to meaningfully consult affected workers, communities, and civil society organisations in due diligence processes.
stakeholder_engagement_requirements.salience: Trade unions push for mandatory worker consultation and collective bargaining recognition; industry prefers voluntary, self-defined engagement. NGOs seek formal access rights for affected communities.

enforcement_and_supervisory_authority.description: Design of national supervisory authorities and the powers they hold to investigate and sanction companies.
enforcement_and_supervisory_authority.salience: Member states resist Commission oversight; industry prefers weak national bodies; civil society demands harmonised and adequately resourced enforcement to prevent a race to the bottom.

sme_indirect_burden.description: How the directive's requirements cascade to SMEs through contractual due diligence demands from larger in-scope companies.
sme_indirect_burden.salience: SME associations warn of de facto compliance burdens without legal obligation. Large firms want contractual pass-through liability; SMEs demand safe-harbour provisions and public support mechanisms.

human_rights_standards_referenced.description: Which international human rights and labour conventions are incorporated as the normative baseline for due diligence obligations.
human_rights_standards_referenced.salience: The choice of baseline instruments determines scope of obligations; NGOs push for comprehensive ILO and UN treaty coverage; industry prefers narrow, well-defined lists to limit legal uncertainty.

environmental_harm_definition.description: The breadth and specificity of environmental damage that triggers due diligence obligations, including biodiversity and pollution.
environmental_harm_definition.salience: Environmental NGOs push for broad biodiversity and ecosystem coverage; extractive and chemical industries resist open-ended definitions that create unpredictable liability exposure.

pair_details.position_summary: IKEA supported CSDDD simplification in principle but opposed restricting due diligence to tier-1 suppliers, arguing it would miss most negative impacts and diverge from OECD and UNDP standards, while also raising concerns about limiting companies' right to seek supply chain information.
pair_details.reasoning: The amendment affirms companies' entitlement to use independent reports and complaints-procedure information, supporting IKEA's concern about preserving companies' right to seek and use supply chain information beyond tier-1.

pair_details.position_summary: IKEA supported CSDDD simplification in principle but opposed restricting due diligence to tier-1 suppliers, arguing it would miss most negative impacts and diverge from OECD and UNDP standards, while also raising concerns about limiting companies' right to seek supply chain information.
pair_details.reasoning: The amendment's focus on director-level stakeholder and civil society consultation does not directly address IKEA's stated concerns about tier-1 scope restrictions or supply chain information rights, making it neutral relative to IKEA's position.
Emerson took no clear stance for or against CSDDD obligations, so the deletion of financial-services due diligence timing cannot be mapped to a directional preference.
Deleting the financial-services due diligence provision reduces the overall scope of CSDDD obligations, aligning with Baker Hughes's call to cut excessive reporting and disclosure requirements.
Removing the financial-services due diligence paragraph weakens mandatory value-chain oversight, directly contradicting the coalition's position against gutting the directive's core protections.
The deletion narrows mandatory due diligence obligations in the financial sector, moving away from the NGOs' active campaign to preserve strong CSDDD requirements.

Deleting the financial-services due diligence timing rule simplifies and reduces the scope of CSDDD obligations, consistent with TotalEnergies's stated desire to lighten corporate sustainability burdens.
Baker Hughes supported decarbonisation goals but opposed CSDDD liability provisions; mandatory director remuneration linked to GHG targets strengthens climate obligations they sought to weaken.
ExxonMobil sought regulatory simplification and reduced burdens; linking director pay to GHG targets adds mandatory climate obligations contrary to their position.

pair_details.position_org: Chamber of Commerce of the United States of America
pair_details.reasoning: AmCham pushed for CSDDD simplification and reduced regulatory burden; mandatory remuneration tied to sustainability targets increases compliance obligations against their stated position.
DHL supports CSDDD and has existing ESG/emissions commitments; linking director remuneration to sustainability and GHG targets aligns with their pro-compliance stance.
The NGOs oppose weakening mandatory due diligence obligations; requiring director pay to be tied to ecological and social sustainability targets strengthens the CSDDD framework they seek to preserve.

The amendment is permissive ('entitled to make use of') rather than imposing mandatory meaningful consultation, and the excerpt is incomplete, making it neutral against Anti-Slavery International's demand for strong stakeholder engagement obligations.

Explicitly requires directors to give 'due consideration for relevant input from stakeholders and civil society organisations,' directly embedding stakeholder engagement into corporate governance in line with Anti-Slavery International's position.

The permissive framing allows companies to use independent reports and complaints data but does not mandate active stakeholder consultation, leaving this neutral relative to the NGOs' demand for robust mandatory engagement.
Director-level accountability for stakeholder and civil society input aligns with the NGOs' push to preserve meaningful consultation obligations within CSDDD.

pair_details.position_org: Clean Clothes Campaign / Stichting Schone Kleren Kampagne
pair_details.reasoning: Granting companies the option to use complaints-procedure data is a weak, permissive measure that does not strengthen the mandatory stakeholder consultation standards Clean Clothes Campaign seeks to protect.

pair_details.position_org: Clean Clothes Campaign / Stichting Schone Kleren Kampagne
pair_details.reasoning: Requiring directors to give 'due consideration' to stakeholder and civil society input moves toward the meaningful consultation obligations Clean Clothes Campaign wants retained in CSDDD.
The permissive right to use independent reports does not introduce or strengthen mandatory consultation with affected communities or rights-holders that Save the Children advocates for.
Embedding stakeholder and civil society input at the director/board level supports Save the Children's position that strong human rights due diligence must include meaningful engagement obligations.

Affirming companies' entitlement to use independent reports and supply chain complaints data directly supports IKEA's concern about preserving companies' right to seek and use information beyond tier-1 suppliers.

Director-level responsibility for stakeholder input is not directly relevant to IKEA's stated concern about supply chain depth and information rights, making this amendment neutral relative to their position.
